mnemonics.title: FIDOE - The Five Algorithm Characteristics
mnemonics.content:
Finiteness → Definiteness → Input → Output → Effectiveness
Mnemonic: 'FIdoe' sounds like 'Fido'—a finite, definite pet that takes input (food) and produces output (joy), effectively! Associate Fido the dog with the five properties in order.

mnemonics.title: Algorithm vs Program vs Process
mnemonics.content:
Algorithm = Recipe (abstract)
Program = Cooked dish (implementation)
Process = Eating experience (execution)
Remember: The recipe (algorithm) exists conceptually; the dish (program) is made from it; the act of eating (process) is the runtime behavior.

mnemonics.title: Definiteness vs Effectiveness Distinction
mnemonics.content:
Definiteness: 'Unambiguous' - only one interpretation
Effectiveness: 'Executable' - can be done in principle
Mnemonic: D for Definition (clear meaning), E for Execution (can do it). A command can be clear (definite) but impossible (not effective)—like 'find the largest prime'.

acronyms.usage: Five essential properties that any computational procedure must satisfy to be called an algorithm. Remember: FIDOE = F-ID-OE (Finiteness, then ID, then OE for easier recall).

acronyms.expansion: A Logical Gathering Of Rational Operations That Handle Mathematics
acronyms.usage: Informal backronym to remember that algorithms are logical, systematic mathematical procedures—not magic, just structured problem-solving.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box labeled 'Algorithm' with arrows going IN (inputs) and arrows going OUT (outputs). Inside, show: [Step 1] → [Step 2] → ... → [Step n] → Output. This reinforces input-output transformation and finite sequence properties.

visualTips.concept: The five characteristics as a cycle
visualTips.visualization: Draw a pentagon with vertices: Finiteness → Definiteness → Input → Output → Effectiveness → back to Finiteness. Show that all five are interconnected—algorithms cycle through these properties in execution.

Show three layers:
Top: ALGORITHM (pseudocode, flowchart)
Middle: PROGRAM (C, Python, Java code)
Bottom: PROCESS (running program, CPU/memory usage)
Arrows show: Algorithm → is implemented as → Program → becomes → Process when executed.

analogies.analogy: Just as a recipe transforms ingredients (input) into a dish (output) through a finite sequence of definite steps, an algorithm transforms input data into output results. Both require the steps to be clear (definite) and doable (effective).

A factory takes raw materials (input), processes them through sequential stations (definite steps), and produces finished goods (output). If any station is ambiguous or impossible, the line fails—mirroring algorithm properties.

Formally, an algorithm is a computable function f: D → R where D is the domain of inputs and R is the range of outputs. Just as functions map every input to exactly one output, algorithms must produce well-defined output for each valid input.
